# Expected findings: unsupported-leap.md

## About this fixture

The piece argues that the growth team should shift effort from retention
experiments to activation funnel work. The premises are individually
defensible: the retention experiments did not move the metric; activation
completion correlates strongly with day-thirty retention. The leap is
between those premises and the operational conclusion that _changing_ the
activation funnel will _cause_ retention to improve. The activation
gradient is a correlation across users; nothing in the piece argues that
moving a marginal user from "completes two steps" to "completes three
steps" will move that user's retention from eleven per cent to forty-one
per cent. That would require the steps themselves to be doing causal work,
rather than activation completion being a signal of underlying user intent.
The missing inferential step — the one the reader is invited to fill from
intuition — is the causal interpretation of the gradient.

## Expected findings (most obvious first)

1. **What:** The argument treats a correlation between activation
   completion and retention as if it licensed a causal intervention. The
   gradient is presented as observational, then used to justify changing
   the funnel as though the change will move users along the gradient.
   **Where:** Paragraph two establishes the gradient; paragraph three makes
   the causal leap ("by day thirty, the user's activation state is already
   locked in"); paragraph four operationalises the leap into a sprint plan.
   **Why it matters:** Users who complete four activation steps may simply
   be the users who were going to retain anyway. Reordering step three may
   move users across the gradient without moving any user's retention. The
   conclusion does not follow without a separate argument that the steps
   cause the retention, not just predict it.

2. **What:** The premise that the retention experiments failed because they
   operate on the wrong cohort is asserted, not argued.
   **Where:** Paragraph three ("The retention experiments have failed to
   move the needle because they are operating on the wrong cohort").
   **Why it matters:** The experiments could have failed for any number of
   reasons — poorly designed treatments, insufficient power, ceiling
   effects, segment heterogeneity. The "wrong cohort" explanation is the
   one that supports the activation pivot, but it is not the only
   explanation consistent with the data presented.

3. **What:** The proposed activation interventions are not connected to a
   mechanism. The piece does not argue why reordering step three would
   change which users complete the funnel, only that step three has the
   highest drop-off.
   **Where:** Paragraph four ("reordering step three (which has the highest
   drop-off)").
   **Why it matters:** Highest drop-off and highest-leverage are different
   properties. A step may have high drop-off because the users who quit
   there were never going to use the product, in which case reordering it
   recovers nobody.

4. **What:** The piece does not consider that the retention experiments and
   the activation work might be complements rather than substitutes.
   **Where:** The framing of paragraph one ("retention experiments... have
   been, in aggregate, unimpressive") into paragraph four ("redirect the
   growth team's next two sprints away from retention").
   **Why it matters:** The proposal is presented as a reallocation forced
   by the data, but the data does not force a reallocation; it is
   consistent with running both.
